chunk_summary: Guidelines for energy-dense complementary feeding in infants aged 6-24 months with chronic cardiorespiratory disease, emphasizing calorie densification, safe textures, and infection prevention.
learning_objectives: Understand the nutritional needs of infants with CHD or CLD during complementary feeding.
Learn strategies for calorie densification in culturally relevant foods for infants aged 6-24 months.
Identify feeding methods and precautions to minimize aspiration and infection risks.
Monitor weight velocity and adjust feeding plans for infants with chronic cardiorespiratory disease.
key_facts: Infants with CHD or CLD have higher energy needs but may experience fatigue limiting intake.
Recommended foods include khichdi, dal-rice, idli, dosa, poha, upma, dalia, and kheer enriched with ghee or oil.
Persistent tachypnea may require prolonged use of thick puree textures to reduce choking risk.
Avoid thin juices and large-volume watery foods that displace calories.
Monthly weight velocity monitoring is essential; adjust density or add supplemental milk feeds if gains fall below expected levels.
Cup-and-spoon feeding is safer than bottle feeding in settings with inconsistent cleaning practices.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: From 6 months, the plan shifts from milk-dominant feeding to an energy-dense mixed diet while maintaining safe textures and minimizing infection. (Indian Academy of Pediatrics, Parental guideline: Complementary feeding)
document_key_topics: Energy-dense complementary feeding for infants with chronic cardiorespiratory disease
Safe feeding textures and methods to prevent aspiration and infection
Monitoring weight velocity and adjusting feeding plans
